source.title: Hierarchical active inference: A theory of motivated control

confidence_rationale: Pezzulo, Rigoli and Friston extend active inference to a hierarchical architecture in which distinct levels of the hierarchy implement different types of predictive processing at different timescales -- fast sensorimotor prediction at lower levels, abstract goal-directed planning at higher levels, with motivational/drive states modulating both. The paper argues that these levels are not equivalent implementations of the same computation: lower levels are dominated by sensory prediction error (fast, reactive); higher levels are dominated by expected free energy minimization over multi-step plans (slow, prospective). Crucially, the paper argues that a flat (non-hierarchical) agent cannot solve tasks requiring both rapid sensory correction and long-horizon planning -- the levels are functionally irreducible. This maps directly onto ARC-025's claim that E1 (fast sensory), E2 (medium action-consequence), and E3 (slow harm/goal planning) cannot be collapsed. The main limitation is that the paper identifies two levels of hierarchy (fast sensorimotor and slow goal-directed) more clearly than three, and the motivational/drive layer is not cleanly separated as a third irreducible level.
failure_signatures: The paper primarily distinguishes two levels of hierarchy (fast sensorimotor and slow goal-directed), not three. The three-engine irreducibility claim requires a third distinct level that is not as clearly established.

Motivational control is treated as a modulator of the two-level hierarchy rather than a separate third engine, which could suggest a two-engine architecture is sufficient with motivational weighting on top.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Hierarchical active inference requires at least two functionally irreducible levels: fast sensorimotor prediction/correction and slow goal-directed planning via expected free energy. A flat single-level agent cannot implement both simultaneously without a hierarchical architecture. Motivational states modulate the precision weighting across levels, enabling context-dependent emphasis on exploration versus exploitation.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-025 claims E1/E2/E3 are a three-way irreducible partition. Pezzulo et al. provide the theoretical support for at least a two-way irreducibility (fast sensory vs slow planning) -- the E1/E3 distinction. The E2 engine (action-consequence forward model, motor-sensory error) occupies an intermediate level that the paper does not clearly separate from either the fast sensorimotor layer or the slow planning layer. This is both a mapping caveat and a substantive pointer: the strongest two-level argument covers E1 vs E3, while E2's irreducibility requires separate grounding (MECH-069 provides this via the incommensurability of motor-sensory vs harm/goal error). The paper therefore partially supports ARC-025 and partially leaves the E2 distinction to other evidence.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper is primarily concerned with the fast/slow hierarchy, not with three levels. The motivational layer is treated as precision weighting over the existing levels rather than as a third irreducible engine. REE's E2 (action-consequence forward model) occupies a middle position that the paper does not cleanly differentiate from the sensorimotor layer -- E2 is faster than E3 planning but slower than E1 sensory inference. Whether E2 constitutes a genuinely irreducible third level or could be implemented as part of a two-level hierarchy with the appropriate architecture is not resolved by this paper.
mapping.source_context: Theoretical review in Trends in Cognitive Sciences; connects to BG-PFC architecture via motivated control; draws on computational psychiatry literature; by core active inference group (Pezzulo, Friston)

confidence_components.notes: High source quality: review in TiCS by leading researchers. Mapping fidelity is below average: the paper supports a two-level hierarchy more clearly than three, and the E2-level irreducibility is not addressed. Transfer risk is moderate-high for the same reason: if the paper's two-level hierarchy is sufficient for viable cognition, a two-engine REE reduction (E1+E3, with E2 as a special case) might be defensible, which would weaken ARC-025.
